questions.prompt: Four defining properties of a Tesla Gigafactory?

choices.text: GWh-scale annual capacity, integrated vertical stack (cells + packs + vehicles + energy products), co-located suppliers, single nervous system (shared software + culture across sites). Without these the 'giga' name is just marketing; with them the operational structure differs from traditional plants.

questions.explanation: The four properties are what make a Gigafactory operationally distinct, not just large. Scale + vertical integration + co-location + shared software.

questions.prompt: Why does early scale matter so disproportionately in battery manufacturing?

choices.text: Wright's Law / experience curves: ~25-30% cost reduction per doubling of cumulative output historically. Being early to scale means riding the curve down faster — a structural advantage that compounds for years. Late entrants face a moving target as Tesla's costs keep dropping.

questions.explanation: High-learning-rate industries reward being first to scale disproportionately. Batteries have steep learning curves; Tesla's lead compounds while competitors catch up.

questions.prompt: Should commodity fasteners be co-located with the Gigafactory?

choices.text: No. Co-location's benefits (logistics, iteration speed, supply continuity) don't justify its costs (reduced competition, real estate commitments, lock-in) on commodities. Reserve co-location for high-volume parts, items needing engineering iteration, and strategic suppliers. Keep commodities competitive: many suppliers, lowest bid, ship from wherever.

questions.explanation: Co-location is selectively valuable. Commodity items don't gain enough to justify the costs of physical co-location and lock-in.

questions.prompt: When does the scale-concentration strategy break down?

choices.text: When demand uncertainty makes large fixed costs risky; when concentration creates political/geographic risk (one country lockdown = large share of output offline); when market maturity arrives and underutilized capacity hurts more than scale helps. Shanghai during COVID was a concrete example of concentration risk hitting.

questions.explanation: Scale wins during growth + learning. As markets mature and risks concentrate, balance shifts toward more geographic diversification and flexibility.

questions.prompt: Why does Tesla make stationary storage (Powerwall, Megapack) at the same Gigafactories as vehicles?

choices.text: Lack of land for separate factories.

Cells are the common platform feeding both. Shared engineering investments benefit both. Capex amortizes across more revenue. Demand cycles differ — storage and vehicle demand don't always move together — smoothing plant utilization. Strategic optionality: allocate cells to whichever product has best margin or demand.

questions.explanation: Cell-as-common-platform with multiple end products is the integration dividend. Shared everything; smoothed demand cycles; strategic re-allocation. Specialized competitors miss these benefits.
